objective: Build a browser-playable Eastern fantasy turn-based artillery prototype whose first screen is the game experience, not a landing page.
context: Greenfield static web game. The worker should optimize the first implementation pass for playable richness and visual clarity: a battlefield, 2-4 local players, class identity, HP/MP, weapons, special abilities, wind, destructible terrain, combat log, and restartable win state. This is a creative prototype handoff, so product completeness and visible feedback matter more than a rigid internal architecture.
scope_breadth: Deliver a full playable prototype with 4 distinct character classes, 4 weapons, at least 1 special ability per class, movement, aiming, firing, terrain destruction, HP/MP updates, turn rotation, combat log, win/restart state, and setup for 2-4 players. Do not ship a minimal two-dot artillery demo.
architecture_freedom: Pin the product experience and acceptance criteria, not the internal architecture. A single-file vanilla JS implementation is acceptable for the prototype. HTML/CSS HUD, menus, cards, sliders, modals, and logs are allowed and encouraged; do not hand-draw UI on canvas unless it improves the result. Keep only correctness-critical calculations such as damage, turn rotation, and projectile stepping isolated enough to inspect or later test.
visual_acceptance: Canvas battlefield occupies at least 60% of the desktop viewport width and 45% of viewport height.
Player characters are clearly legible at 1280x720: each character body is at least 28px tall or 6% of battlefield height, whichever is larger.
HUD uses HTML/CSS cards or panels for every player with readable HP and MP text; main controls are at least 40px tall.
Background is visually rich: at minimum a 2-color sky gradient plus moon/stars/clouds or equivalent themed detail, not a flat fill.
Terrain has at least 3 visually distinct layers or textures, and projectile impacts visibly alter the terrain.
Projectile trail remains visible for at least 400ms and explosion/damage feedback remains visible for at least 300ms.
Theme is recognizable as Eastern fantasy through names, palette, icons/silhouettes, and on-screen labels.
reference_assets: Described density: one-screen game layout with top player cards, central battlefield, bottom control panels, and floating combat log, comparable in information density to a small Worms/Gunbound-style browser prototype.

criteria.requirement: The first loaded screen is immediately playable or one click from play, with battlefield, HUD, controls, and combat log visible.
criteria.effect: A user can understand whose turn it is, select an action, aim, and fire without reading external documentation.

criteria.requirement: Supports 2, 3, and 4 local hot-seat players with distinct class identity.
criteria.evidence: Manual setup audit for each player count.
criteria.effect: Player cards, character markers, HP/MP values, and turns match the selected count.

criteria.requirement: Firing a weapon changes game state and visible feedback.
criteria.evidence: Manual audit: fire once and observe projectile, terrain impact or miss, MP cost, log entry, and turn action lockout.
criteria.effect: MP decreases, action buttons update, combat log records the shot, and the battlefield shows projectile or impact feedback.

criteria.requirement: Damage, elimination, turn rotation, and win/restart work.
criteria.effect: Damaged players lose HP, eliminated players are skipped, one survivor produces a win state, and restart resets state.

boundaries.expected: Only the selected number of players appear and turns rotate through living players only.

boundaries.case: Player lacks enough MP for a selected weapon or ability.
boundaries.expected: Action is blocked or disabled, no negative MP occurs, and the combat log explains the reason.

boundaries.case: Projectile misses all players or exits the battlefield.
boundaries.expected: No damage is applied, a miss/resolution is logged, and the player can end or advance the turn.
Battlefield, HUD, and controls remain usable without text overlap; controls may stack but remain at least 40px tall.

manual_checks: Serve the directory with `python3 -m http.server 8000` and open http://127.0.0.1:8000.
Start 4-player play, fire one weapon, use one special ability, end a turn, and verify visible state updates.
Inspect console for errors during load, firing, ability use, turn end, and restart.
Resize to mobile portrait and verify the Visual & UX Quality checklist.

validation_commands.proves: The main JavaScript file is syntactically valid.

worker_steps: Create the static files and implement a high-density first screen with battlefield, player cards, controls, and log.
Implement classes, weapons, abilities, HP/MP, turn state, movement, wind, projectile firing, terrain destruction, and restart.
Add visual feedback for aiming, firing, projectile travel, impact, damage, disabled actions, and turn change.
Run the syntax check and manual browser checklist, then report acceptance evidence with screenshots or concise observations.
anti_patterns: Do not invent imports, helper APIs, frameworks, build tools, package scripts, or external services; use plain static files unless the user explicitly changes scope.
Do not edit tests, validation commands, or manual acceptance criteria merely to hide production failures.
Do not build a landing page or static mockup instead of the playable game.
Do not reduce the scope to two generic players, one weapon, no abilities, or no combat log.
Do not force HUD, menus, sliders, cards, or logs into canvas rendering when HTML/CSS would be clearer.
Do not add npm dependencies, bundlers, frameworks, accounts, backends, online multiplayer, or generated build artifacts.
Do not report success without the syntax check and manual browser audit evidence.
phase_decomposition_rationale: Single-lane prototype is intentional because this is a first-pass creative game build. A later handoff can split pure logic tests and refactoring after the playable prototype exists.
hidden_public_alignment: No owner-only checks are configured. Visual quality, scope breadth, and gameplay behavior are represented as public manual acceptance criteria and manual audit items.
